claim: For all integers n >= 1, the exponent of 2 in (2n)! is at least n.
For all integers n >= 1, the 2-adic valuation of n! is equal to n minus the sum of its digits in binary.
For all integers n >= 1, the 2-adic valuation of (2n)! is exactly 2n minus the sum of the digits of n in binary.
The 2-adic valuation of (2n)! is exactly n plus the 2-adic valuation of n!.
The 2-adic valuation of the central binomial coefficient C(2n, n) is equal to the sum of the digits of n in binary.
For all integers n >= 1, the number of '1's in the binary representation of n is at least 1.
For all integers n >= 1, the central binomial coefficient C(2n, n) is an even integer.
The 2-adic valuation of n! is strictly less than n for all n >= 1.
The 2-adic valuation of n! is exactly n - 1 if and only if n is a power of 2.
Any two consecutive integers n and n+1 are relatively prime.
Euler's totient function phi(n) is at most n - 1 for all integers n > 1.
For all integers n > 1, phi(n) = n - 1 if and only if n is a prime number.
The sum of divisors function sigma(n) is at least n + 1 for all integers n > 1.
For all integers n > 1, sigma(n) = n + 1 if and only if n is a prime number.
The number of divisors tau(n) is equal to 2 if and only if n is prime.
If n is a prime number, then the sum of its Euler totient and its divisor sum is exactly 2n.
An integer n > 1 is prime if and only if the sum of its totient and divisor sum is n times its divisor count.
The central binomial coefficient C(2n, n) is always divisible by n + 1, resulting in the n-th Catalan number.
The 2-adic valuation of the n-th Catalan number is the binary digit sum of n minus the 2-adic valuation of n + 1.
The n-th Catalan number is an odd integer if and only if n is of the form 2^k - 1.
Legendre's formula states that for any prime p, the p-adic valuation of n! is (n - s_p(n)) / (p - 1).

The greatest common divisor of n and (n-1)! + 1 is equal to n if and only if n is prime.

The sum of divisors of a perfect square is always an odd integer.
